title: Oil tank removal cost direction
summary: Cross-state guide for directional cost talk after the trigger state and route are already clear.
quickAnswer: Cost direction only becomes honest after the route is clear enough to separate sweep, closure, removal, and cleanup.
stateEntryPrompt: Choose a state route for cost direction.
introPoints: Keep cost directional and tied to a clear scenario.
Use state guidance before treating a market article as authoritative.
Move back to verification routes when the facts are still thin.
whatNotToAssume: Do not treat a broad range as a quote.
Do not assume the same cost logic applies to sweep, removal, and cleanup.
Do not use cost direction to skip verification.
bestFor: The route is already narrow enough that the user needs directional cost, not a generic article.
Tank presence, closure path, or cleanup path is clearer than it was at the first trigger.
The user can already separate sweep, closure, removal, and cleanup scenarios.
beforeYouCall: Lock the scenario first: sweep, closure, removal, or cleanup.
Pull any state cost reference or fund guidance before treating a contractor number as the whole answer.
Keep the range directional until the record stack and site facts are stronger.
documentsThatMatter: State cost references, fund guidance, and any official closure or cleanup benchmark.
Sweep, closure, removal, or site-assessment records showing which scenario actually applies.
Any contamination or access fact that materially widens the range.
whenToEscalate: Move back to sweep-first or records-first if the scenario is still not verified.
Move to leak-and-cleanup when contamination concern, remediation, or reporting language appears.
Stay state-specific because the same range logic does not travel cleanly across states.
